name: Patanjali
dates_notes: Two scholarly families of dating co-exist. The traditional identification with Patañjali the grammarian (author of the Mahābhāṣya, ~150 BCE) yields a second-century BCE date and is defended by a minority (Liebich; some Indian scholars). The dominant modern position (Larson; Maas 2013; Bronkhorst) treats the Yoga-Sūtra-author as distinct from the grammarian, and dates the sūtras to the early centuries CE — Maas argues for a third-to-fourth-century-CE composition with the Vyāsa-Bhāṣya as a near-contemporaneous auto-commentary or redaction. Range given here covers both readings.

lineage_polemical.note: Brahma-Sūtra 2.1.3 (etena yogaḥ pratyuktaḥ) extends the Sāṃkhya-refutation of the previous adhikaraṇa to Yoga, on the grounds that Yoga shares the Sāṃkhya pradhāna-kāraṇa-vāda; the sustained engagement is left to the bhāṣyakāras.

Vijñānabhikṣu's Yoga-Vārttika is the principal late-medieval re-engagement with the Pātañjala-Yoga-Śāstra from a Vedāntic harmonising standpoint; the relation is constructive on praxis, polemical on īśvara-as-puruṣa-viśeṣa.

core_thesis: The *Yoga-Sūtra* compresses an entire darśana into 195 *sūtras* across four *pādas* — *Samādhi*, *Sādhana*, *Vibhūti*, *Kaivalya*. Its definitional thesis is given in the second *sūtra*: *yogaḥ citta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ* — yoga is the cessation of the modifications of the *citta*. The metaphysics is Sāṃkhya in inheritance: *puruṣa* and *prakṛti* as ontologically distinct, the *guṇas* as constitutive of *prakṛti*, *satkārya-vāda* on causation. The Yoga additions are doctrinally consequential. *Īśvara* is admitted as a *puruṣa-viśeṣa* (special *puruṣa*) untouched by *kleśa-karma-vipāka-āśaya* — the affliction-action-fruition-deposit chain — and *īśvara-praṇidhāna* is one efficient route to *samādhi*. The eight *aṅgas* (*yama*, *niyama*, *āsana*, *prāṇāyāma*, *pratyāhāra*, *dhāraṇā*, *dhyāna*, *samādhi*) supply a graded praxis. *Kaivalya* — the *puruṣa*'s isolation from *prakṛti* once the *guṇas* have been reabsorbed — is the *mokṣa*-doctrine. The author is conventionally identified with Patañjali the grammarian, but the modern majority view (Larson; Maas) treats the two as distinct. The text's standard intra-traditional reading is mediated by the *Vyāsa-Bhāṣya* and its sub-commentaries (*Tattva-Vaiśāradī*, *Yoga-Vārttika*).

engaged_works.title: Yoga-Sutra
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composition-date depends on whether Patañjali-the-grammarian and Patañjali-the-Yoga-Sūtra-author are the same person; the modern majority view dates the sūtras to the early centuries CE.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Uniformly ascribed to Patañjali by the colophon-tradition and by every later Yoga commentator. The historical-author question (identity with the grammarian; relation to Vyāsa-Bhāṣya) is open in modern scholarship but does not affect the ascription's traditional status.

ascription_evidence.description: Maas (2013, 'A Concise Historiography of Classical Yoga Philosophy') argues sūtras and bhāṣya should be treated as a single Pātañjala-Yoga-Śāstra; Larson (1989, Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies vol. XII) treats them as distinct. Either way the sūtras are securely ascribed to Patañjali.

engaged_works.summary: Four *pādas* compressing classical Yoga into 195 *sūtras*. The *Samādhi-Pāda* opens with the definition *yogaḥ citta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ* and unfolds the typology of the five *vṛttis*, the disciplines of *abhyāsa* and *vairāgya*, the doctrine of *samprajñāta-* and *asamprajñāta-samādhi*, and the brief but consequential *īśvara-pāda* introducing *īśvara-praṇidhāna* as a *samādhi*-route. The *Sādhana-Pāda* gives *kriyā-yoga* (*tapas*, *svādhyāya*, *īśvara-praṇidhāna*), the analysis of the five *kleśas*, and the first five of the eight *aṅgas* — *yama*, *niyama*, *āsana*, *prāṇāyāma*, *pratyāhāra*. The *Vibhūti-Pāda* gives *dhāraṇā*, *dhyāna*, *samādhi* (the last three *aṅgas*, jointly *saṃyama*) and the *vibhūtis* generated by *saṃyama* on various objects, with the warning at 3.37 that they are obstacles to *samādhi*. The *Kaivalya-Pāda* gives the metaphysics of liberation: *puruṣa*'s isolation from *prakṛti* once the *guṇas* have completed their work for it, with discussion of *dharma-megha-samādhi* and the *citta*'s reabsorption into its source. The standard pre-modern reading is mediated by the *Vyāsa-Bhāṣya* and the sub-commentaries of Vāchaspati Miśra (*Tattva-Vaiśāradī*) and Vijñānabhikṣu (*Yoga-Vārttika*).
